IoT-Based Air Quality Monitoring & Pollution Control System

Real-time monitoring, smart automation, and sustainable environmental protection

“When the air we breathe becomes a threat, innovation becomes our responsibility.”

Introduction

Air pollution has become one of the most critical environmental and health challenges worldwide, especially in rapidly growing urban areas. Exposure to harmful gases such as carbon monoxide (CO), carbon dioxide (CO₂), nitrogen oxides (NOx), and particulate matter (PM2.5, PM10) can lead to serious respiratory and cardiovascular diseases. Traditional air quality monitoring systems are expensive, bulky, and provide limited real-time data, making them unsuitable for widespread deployment. To address this issue, this project proposes an IoT-based Air Quality Monitoring and Pollution Control System that is low-cost, efficient, and scalable. The system uses an ESP32 microcontroller integrated with multiple gas and environmental sensors to continuously monitor air quality parameters. Data is transmitted to cloud platforms for real-time visualization and analysis. Additionally, the system includes an automated control mechanism that activates fans or purifiers when pollution levels exceed safe thresholds. This solution not only monitors environmental conditions but also takes corrective actions, making it a smart and proactive system for improving air quality in homes, industries, and smart cities.

Problem Statement

Air pollution has emerged as a major environmental and public health concern, particularly in urban and industrial regions. Exposure to harmful pollutants such as carbon monoxide (CO), nitrogen dioxide (NO₂), sulfur dioxide (SO₂), and particulate matter (PM2.5 and PM10) can lead to severe respiratory diseases, cardiovascular problems, and long-term health risks.

Conventional air quality monitoring systems are expensive, bulky, and require specialized infrastructure, limiting their deployment to a few fixed locations. These systems often fail to provide real-time data and lack accessibility for common users, resulting in delayed response to hazardous conditions.

Moreover, most existing solutions focus only on monitoring and do not provide any mechanism for automatic control or mitigation of pollution levels. This creates a gap between detection and action, reducing the effectiveness of such systems.

Therefore, there is a need for a low-cost, real-time, and intelligent air quality monitoring system that not only detects multiple pollutants but also takes immediate corrective actions to improve air quality.

Proposed Solution

The proposed system is a smart IoT-based air quality monitoring and pollution control system designed to provide real-time environmental data and automatic corrective actions. It integrates multiple gas and environmental sensors with an ESP32 microcontroller to continuously monitor air quality parameters.

The collected data is processed and transmitted to cloud platforms such as ThingSpeak or Blynk for real-time visualization and analysis. When pollutant levels exceed predefined safe thresholds, the system automatically activates control mechanisms such as exhaust fans or air purifiers using relay modules.

This system transforms traditional passive monitoring into an active pollution control solution, making it suitable for homes, industries, and smart city applications.

Sensors

Detect harmful gases like CO2 and smoke.

ESP32

ESP32 with Sensors Integration. It processes and sends data to cloud.

Automation

Controls fans/purifiers automatically.

Methodology

The working of the proposed IoT-based air quality monitoring system is divided into multiple stages, ensuring accurate data collection, processing, and automatic control.

1. Data Acquisition

Sensors such as MQ-series gas sensors, DHT11, and BME280 continuously monitor environmental parameters like gas concentration, temperature, humidity, and pressure.

2. Data Processing

The ESP32 microcontroller collects analog signals from sensors, converts them into digital values, and calculates pollutant concentration levels (PPM).

3. Data Transmission

Processed data is transmitted to cloud platforms such as ThingSpeak or Blynk using Wi-Fi for remote monitoring and storage.

4. Data Visualization

Users can view real-time air quality data through dashboards, graphs, and mobile applications.

5. Threshold Detection

The system continuously compares sensor values with predefined safe limits to detect abnormal conditions.

6. Automatic Control

When pollution levels exceed safe thresholds, the system activates a relay to turn on exhaust fans or purifiers to reduce pollution.

Figure: Block Diagram / Working Flow of the System

Device Working and Results

The IoT-based Air Quality Monitoring System operates as a real-time embedded system that continuously senses environmental conditions and responds automatically to unsafe air quality levels.

Initially, gas sensors such as MQ3 and MQ6 detect the presence of harmful gases like alcohol vapors, LPG, and other pollutants. Environmental sensors like DHT11 and BME280 measure temperature, humidity, and atmospheric pressure. These sensors generate analog signals based on pollutant concentration.

The ESP32 microcontroller collects this sensor data and converts it into digital values. It then processes the data to determine the concentration levels (in ppm) and compares them with predefined threshold values.

If the detected pollution level exceeds the safe limit (for example, above a threshold value of 70), the system identifies it as an abnormal condition. The ESP32 then activates a relay module, which automatically turns ON devices such as an exhaust fan or air purifier to reduce pollution levels.

Simultaneously, the data is transmitted to cloud platforms like ThingSpeak or Blynk using Wi-Fi. This allows users to monitor air quality remotely through dashboards and mobile applications.

An LCD display connected to the system provides real-time local feedback by showing parameters such as gas concentration, temperature, and humidity.

Once the air quality returns to safe levels, the system automatically turns OFF the control devices, completing a closed-loop pollution control system.
